title: Track every pound coming in for a week
subtitle: A seven-day tracking activity for shifting attention from what leaves to what arrives.
excerpt: A seven-day tracking activity: log every inflow of money for a week, including small and unexpected amounts. The point is not budgeting — it is the shift in attention. Most money anxiety tracks outflows. This activity asks the mind to track inflows with the same attention, building a record the body can reference.
sourceNotes: Income-tracking as a money-mindset practice is documented in folk-prosperity traditions and modern money-mindset work, including Rebecca J Page's The Money Zone (2024). The attention-to-inflows focus is adapted from Day 3 of MONEY: A 12-Week Tapping Program (Rebecca J Page, 2025).

practice.whenToUse: Use during a week when money anxiety is high and the attention is locked on outflows. Best started at the beginning of the week for a clean seven-day record.

content.text: A seven-day tracking activity: log every inflow of money for a week, no matter how small. Salary, client payment, a refund, interest, an unexpected tenner. The point is not budgeting — it's the shift in attention. Most money anxiety tracks what leaves. This activity asks the mind to track what arrives with the same attention, building a record the body can reference when the bills-always-win feeling returns.
A small notebook or the notes app on the phone — whichever you'll actually check daily.
Five minutes each evening for a brief logging session.

Open the notebook to a fresh page. Head it: "Money that arrived this week." On day one, check the bank account and list any inflows from the past few days to seed the list. Then put the notebook somewhere you'll see it at the end of each day.

Open the notebook. Log everything that arrived today: the salary transfer, the client payment, the refund from the supermarket, the interest on the savings account, the friend who paid you back a tenner, the parking refund. Everything. Log the amount and a one-word note for what it was.
On days when nothing arrives: write "nothing today" and close it. The log is complete on those days too.
At the end of the week, add up the total. The total is usually larger than the anxious mind expected. Most money anxiety undercounts inflows and overcounts outflows. The list is the counter-evidence.
Keep the list where you can find it. When the bills-always-win feeling arrives in a later week, read the list from this one.

Income-tracking as a money-mindset practice is documented in folk-prosperity traditions and modern money-mindset work, including The Money Zone (Rebecca Page, 2024). The attention-to-inflows focus is adapted from Day 3 of MONEY: A 12-Week Tapping Program (Rebecca J Page, 2025), where the bills-always-win stress is addressed alongside gratitude for what money already provides.
